caveats: a: The occupation source is 2016 and four-digit rather than current six-digit data.
a: The process-control example concerns flat glass and is only an adjacent technology proxy.
rho: No representative 327212 adoption study was found.
rho: The estimate applies only to the exposed opportunity in a, not total labor or general productivity.
e: The injected n of 51 is an ESTIMATE based on receipts and a sector margin rather than observed firm EBITDA.
e: Product and channel heterogeneity makes establishment-level industry coding a weak proxy for firm eligibility.
s5: The numerator and denominator for qualifying transfers are both unobserved.
s5: Consumer glass and industrial specialty glass likely have materially different buyer universes.
q: No contract-level pass-through study was available.
q: Retention is likely higher in qualified technical components than in undifferentiated tableware or lighting glass.
d5: Four-digit production can diverge from six-digit domestic demand because of trade, inventory, and product mix.
d5: The historical index level does not directly measure a five-year forward demand ratio.
o: This is judgment rather than a measured quantity.
o: Automation can lower labor intensity substantially without eliminating the accountable operating firm, which remains required under this construct.

envelope.interval_scope: research-input sensitivity with dataset anchors (l, n) held fixed

identity.title: Other Pressed and Blown Glass and Glassware Manufacturing

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Repeat business-to-business orders for specified glassware or components, commonly priced by unit, batch, tooling program, or supply agreement, with revenue primarily from manufactured products.
lens.deviation_from_default: The code combines contract-like industrial and technical glass production with consumer tableware and one-off art glass. The lens retains repeat specification-driven B2B manufacturing and excludes retail-brand and one-off artistic models so customer economics and transferability are coherent; the narrowing is based on business-model incompatibility, not attractiveness.
lens.excluded_activities: Branded consumer tableware sold principally through retail, one-off studio or art glass, captive internal plants, glass packaging containers, pure distributors, and firms whose only activity is installation are excluded.
lens.included_activities: U.S. lower-middle-market manufacturers that repeatedly make pressed, blown, or otherwise shaped non-container glassware and glass components to industrial or commercial customer specifications, including laboratory, technical, lighting, appliance, and specialty applications.

a.bridge: BLS reports occupations for four-digit 327200 glass and glass products rather than the narrowed 327212 population or wage-weighted AI task exposure. I map planning, quoting, documentation, engineering analysis, inspection triage, and some machine monitoring to exposure and heavily discount physical forming, furnace, maintenance, packing, and material handling; the broader group includes more continuous-process segments, making the direction of bias uncertain.
caveats: The occupation source is 2016 and four-digit rather than current six-digit data.
The process-control example concerns flat glass and is only an adjacent technology proxy.
a.change_evidence: A current payroll-by-occupation and task-time sample from at least ten independent 327212 plants, separated into laboratory, lighting, technical, and other products and mapped to O*NET tasks, would materially change the range.
a.rationale: The broad glass industry was 55.08% production occupations by employment in 2016, with management 3.97%, business and financial operations 2.22%, sales 2.35%, and office support 8.50%. AI and machine vision can assist quality, scheduling, technical documentation, customer service, and process decisions, but much wage effort remains embodied in physical forming, changeovers, handling, finishing, and maintenance.

d5.bridge: The Federal Reserve/FRED series measures real output for all four-digit 3272 glass and glass products, not constant-quality demand for the narrowed 327212 B2B population. I use its subdued recent level as a cyclical anchor and widen for end-market and product-mix differences; containers and flat glass in the source make the direction of error ambiguous.
caveats: Four-digit production can diverge from six-digit domestic demand because of trade, inventory, and product mix.
The historical index level does not directly measure a five-year forward demand ratio.
d5.change_evidence: Constant-dollar 327212 shipments and import penetration by product class, combined with customer production forecasts for laboratory, lighting, appliance, and technical applications, would materially change the five-year ratio.
d5.rationale: The broad sector's industrial-production index was 94.2941 in May 2026 on a 2017=100 basis, providing no evidence of a broad structural surge. Essential laboratory, technical, appliance, and lighting uses support persistence, while imports, plastics and ceramics substitution, LED redesign, customer offshoring, and cyclicality create downside.

caveats: The injected n of 51 is an ESTIMATE based on receipts and a sector margin rather than observed firm EBITDA.
Product and channel heterogeneity makes establishment-level industry coding a weak proxy for firm eligibility.
e.change_evidence: A firm-level classification using D&B Hoovers, Thomasnet, company catalogs, customer concentration, revenue by channel, ownership, and audited normalized EBITDA would materially change the eligible share.
e.rationale: Eligibility is reduced because the NAICS code mixes the retained repeat B2B contract population with consumer tableware, artistic work, captive operations, and businesses without a standalone transferable customer base. Among receipts-bridged LMM candidates, technical and industrial specialists are plausible fits, but normalized EBITDA, customer recurrence, and control independence remain unobserved.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 327212 ($827,918,412) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 327212 ($3,726,067,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.1465 (margins.json[32]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Paper/Forest Products EBITDA/Sales 14.65% (mid-range for wood/paper/plastics)); revenue/employee $326,418 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 327212); class <5: 199 firms, avg revenue/firm $220,000 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $32,230 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 50 firms, avg revenue/firm $824,340 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $120,766 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 42 firms, avg revenue/firm $2,122,905 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $311,006 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 38 firms, avg revenue/firm $10,031,632 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $1,469,634 -> IN $1-10M band; class 100-499: 13 firms, avg revenue/firm $29,737,308 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $4,356,516 -> IN $1-10M band; class 500+: 24 firms, avg revenue/firm $116,005,042 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $16,994,739 -> out of $1-10M band; n_band = 51

caveats: This is judgment rather than a measured quantity.
Automation can lower labor intensity substantially without eliminating the accountable operating firm, which remains required under this construct.
o.change_evidence: Commercial evidence from highly automated 327212 plants showing staffing by line before and after robotics, machine vision, and advanced controls, plus customer qualification requirements for unattended production, would materially move this estimate.
o.rationale: Customers still require a legally and operationally accountable manufacturer to form, anneal, inspect, certify, package, and deliver physical glass. AI can reduce touches and improve controls but cannot convert the current glassware basket into pure software or customer self-service within five years.

caveats: No contract-level pass-through study was available.
Retention is likely higher in qualified technical components than in undifferentiated tableware or lighting glass.
q.change_evidence: A representative contract and quote-history sample showing renewal cadence, cost-escalation clauses, tooling ownership, qualification costs, contribution margins, and price response after measured productivity changes would materially move retention.
q.rationale: Specification qualification, tooling, quality history, and delivery reliability can make supplier switching costly, supporting retention of part of an implemented benefit. However recurring bids, annual price negotiations, should-cost analysis, and competing domestic or imported glass transmit savings to customers over time.

rho.bridge: DOE documents machine-learning control in a large flat-glass furnace, not implementation across smaller pressed and blown glass firms. It establishes feasibility for sensor-rich glass processes, then I discount for product variability, older equipment, short runs, limited data engineering, safety and quality validation, and smaller-firm capital constraints; the large-project context likely biases adoption upward.
caveats: No representative 327212 adoption study was found.
The estimate applies only to the exposed opportunity in a, not total labor or general productivity.
rho.change_evidence: A plant survey covering vision systems, MES/ERP maturity, connected equipment, AI pilots, validation failure rates, capital budgets, and realized labor hours saved by size and product family would materially move the estimate.
rho.rationale: Core applications in vision inspection, scheduling, recipe support, maintenance, quoting, and documentation can be deployed within five years, especially where production is repetitive. Implementation will be uneven because this code contains batch, craft-intensive, and high-mix plants whose data are sparse and whose physical workflows require accountable skilled operators.

caveats: The numerator and denominator for qualifying transfers are both unobserved.
Consumer glass and industrial specialty glass likely have materially different buyer universes.
s5.change_evidence: A five-year ownership-change census from PitchBook, CapIQ, Axial, GF Data, state corporate records, and plant permits, verified by seller interviews and excluding closures, asset-only liquidations, and internal reorganizations, would materially change the estimate.
s5.rationale: No qualifying-transfer cohort specific to independent LMM 327212 firms was found. Specialized industrial firms can attract strategic buyers and succession sales, but family ownership, customer qualification, skilled-worker dependence, environmental exposure, bespoke equipment, and thin buyer pools reduce five-year transfer frequency.

principal_driver: Repeat qualified production offers practical AI-assisted quality, scheduling, engineering, and administrative workflows while preserving the need for a physical operator.
principal_weakness: NAICS heterogeneity and the absence of firm-level eligibility and transaction evidence make the transferable target population highly uncertain.
